text: The surgeon performs pelvic nodal dissection following the medial to lateral mobilization of the rectal tube. The lateral peritoneal cuts are extended over the external iliac vessels, which are identified and fully dissected. The ureter is then mobilized medially to aid in nodal clearance.

The surgeon creates a paravesical space to access the nodal zone between the obturator internus muscle and the internal iliac vasculature. The obliterated umbilical artery is traced downwards to reach the internal iliac artery.

The surgeon reflects the fibrofatty tissue containing lymph nodes medially from the external iliac vessels. Subsequently, the nodal tissue is carefully separated and reflected off the obturator internus muscle.

The surgeon separates the nodal tissue from the bladder fat pad and dissects it away from the confluence of the external and internal iliac vessels. The final step involves dissecting the nodal tissue from the obturator nerve to clear the nodal zone.

The surgeon clears the nodal zone without sacrificing the obturator artery. The second nodal zone, located between the internal iliac vasculature and the hypogastric plexus, is gently opened while preserving the hypogastric plexus.

The surgeon carefully dissects the node-bearing fatty tissue away from the branches of the internal iliac artery, extending the dissection below the inferior vesicle artery due to the planned total pelvic exenteration for advanced malignancy.
